title: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney
authors: Alfred L. George, Jr., Eric G. Neilson

items.subtopic: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney | EMBRYOLOGIC DEVELOPMENT
items.title: Impact of Birth Weight on Nephron Number and Associated Risks
items.content: The number of nephrons in each kidney is determined by branching events during embryologic development. Normal-birth-weight adults typically have ~900,000 glomeruli per kidney, whereas low-birth-weight adults may have as few as 225,000 glomeruli.
keyPoints: Normal-birth-weight adults: ~900,000 glomeruli per kidney
Low-birth-weight adults: ~225,000 glomeruli per kidney
Reduced nephron number in low-birth-weight individuals is associated with increased comorbid risks
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney | EMBRYOLOGIC DEVELOPMENT

items.subtopic: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney | EMBRYOLOGIC DEVELOPMENT
items.title: Role of VEGF-A and Angiopoietin-1 in Glomerular Development
items.content: VEGF-A and angiopoietin-1 are crucial for guiding the development of glomeruli by influencing endothelial cells to form capillaries with surrounding mesangial cells, which differentiate into a glomerular filter.
keyPoints: VEGF-A and angiopoietin-1 guide the formation of glomerular filters
Endothelial cells form capillaries under their influence
Mesangial cells differentiate to support the glomerular structure
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney | EMBRYOLOGIC DEVELOPMENT

items.subtopic: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney | EMBRYOLOGIC DEVELOPMENT
items.title: Differences in Cortical vs. Juxtamedullary Nephrons
items.content: Cortical nephrons, which are more numerous, have short loops of Henle and perform most glomerular filtration. Juxtamedullary nephrons have long loops of Henle, creating an osmotic gradient for urine concentration.
keyPoints: Cortical nephrons: Short loops of Henle, larger afferent arterioles
Juxtamedullary nephrons: Long loops of Henle, vasa recta capillaries
Cortical nephrons perform most glomerular filtration
Juxtamedullary nephrons are crucial for urine concentration
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney | EMBRYOLOGIC DEVELOPMENT

items.subtopic: DETERMINANTS AND REGULATION OF GLOMERULAR FILTRATION
items.title: Autoregulation of Glomerular Filtration Rate (GFR)
items.content: The kidney maintains a stable GFR despite fluctuations in systemic blood pressure through autoregulatory mechanisms involving the myogenic reflex, tubuloglomerular feedback (TGF), and angiotensin II-mediated efferent arteriole constriction.
keyPoints: Renal blood flow is approximately 1000 mL/min, representing ~20% of cardiac output.
Autoregulation involves the myogenic reflex, TGF, and angiotensin II effects.
The myogenic reflex adjusts afferent arteriolar tone in response to changes in renal perfusion pressure.
TGF is mediated by macula densa cells sensing solute concentration; adenosine plays a key role as a vasoconstrictor.
Angiotensin II constricts the efferent arteriole, increasing glomerular hydrostatic pressure and GFR.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · DETERMINANTS AND REGULATION OF GLOMERULAR FILTRATION

items.subtopic: DETERMINANTS AND REGULATION OF GLOMERULAR FILTRATION
items.title: Role of Hydrostatic and Oncotic Pressures in Glomerular Filtration
items.content: Glomerular filtration is driven by a hydrostatic pressure gradient across the glomerular capillary wall, which is opposed by oncotic pressure from plasma proteins.
keyPoints: Hydrostatic pressure is the primary force driving glomerular filtration.
Oncotic pressure opposes filtration and rises along the length of the glomerular capillary.
~20% of renal plasma flow is filtered into Bowman space, defining the filtration fraction.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · DETERMINANTS AND REGULATION OF GLOMERULAR FILTRATION

items.subtopic: DETERMINANTS AND REGULATION OF GLOMERULAR FILTRATION
items.title: Tubuloglomerular Feedback (TGF) Mechanism
items.content: TGF modulates glomerular filtration by adjusting afferent arteriolar tone based on solute delivery to the macula densa, with adenosine acting as a key mediator.
keyPoints: Macula densa cells in the thick ascending limb sense NaCl concentration and flow rate.
High tubular flow increases solute delivery, triggering adenosine-mediated vasoconstriction of the afferent arteriole.
Adenosine is produced from ATP released by macula densa cells during increased NaCl reabsorption.
TGF can be enhanced by angiotensin II and reactive oxygen species or blunted by nitric oxide.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · DETERMINANTS AND REGULATION OF GLOMERULAR FILTRATION

items.subtopic: MECHANISMS OF RENAL TUBULAR TRANSPORT | EPITHELIAL SOLUTE TRANSPORT
items.title: Understanding Polarization in Renal Epithelial Cells for Directional Transport
items.content: Renal epithelial cells are polarized, which allows them to asymmetrically allocate membrane proteins and lipids. This polarization is crucial for the directional movement of fluid and solutes across different segments of the nephron.
keyPoints: Tight junctions create an occlusive barrier separating the tubular lumen from interstitial spaces.
Cells are polarized, with distinct apical (facing lumen) and basolateral (facing interstitium) membranes.
Asymmetric assignment of membrane proteins facilitates directional transport.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · MECHANISMS OF RENAL TUBULAR TRANSPORT | EPITHELIAL SOLUTE TRANSPORT

items.subtopic: MECHANISMS OF RENAL TUBULAR TRANSPORT | EPITHELIAL SOLUTE TRANSPORT
items.title: Differentiating Cellular and Paracellular Transport in Renal Tubules
items.content: Renal tubular epithelial transport occurs via two mechanisms: cellular transport, involving movement across cell membranes mediated by transporters, channels, or pumps; and paracellular transport, which involves movement through tight junctions between cells.
keyPoints: Cellular transport moves solutes sequentially across apical and basolateral membranes.
Paracellular transport occurs through tight junctions, allowing some fluid and solute passage.
Proximal tubules have leaky epithelia for bulk reabsorption; distal segments like the collecting duct have tight epithelia for regulated transport.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · MECHANISMS OF RENAL TUBULAR TRANSPORT | EPITHELIAL SOLUTE TRANSPORT

items.title: Mechanisms of Solute and Water Transport Across Kidney Cell Membranes
items.content: Cell membranes utilize integral membrane proteins such as channels, pumps, transporters, and cotransporters to mediate solute and water movement. Active transport requires ATP to create ion gradients, while passive transport relies on concentration gradients or electrochemical potential.
keyPoints: Active transport involves Na+/K+-ATPase, H+-ATPases, and Ca2+-ATPases, which move ions against their chemical gradient using metabolic energy from ATP hydrolysis.
Passive transport occurs through channels allowing solutes or water to move down concentration gradients.
Facilitated diffusion is mediated by carriers like GLUT2 for glucose, driven by extracellular fluid's higher glucose concentration compared to cytoplasm.
Secondary active transport uses ion gradients (e.g., Na+) to drive other transport mechanisms.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · MEMBRANE TRANSPORT

items.title: Regulation of Renal Tubular Transport in Response to Fluid Flow
items.content: Renal epithelial cells adjust solute and water transport based on tubular fluid flow, using cellular structures like microvilli and mechanosensing ion channels. Changes in intracellular Ca2+ levels or purinergic receptor-mediated signaling regulate this process.
keyPoints: Tubular fluid flow rate is highly variable, necessitating dynamic adjustment of ion transport.
Renal epithelial cells monitor tubular fluid flow using structures such as apical microvilli and primary cilia.
Transport regulation involves changes in intracellular Ca2+ or purinergic receptor-mediated signal transduction.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · MEMBRANE TRANSPORT

items.title: Proximal Tubule Reabsorption Capacity
items.content: The proximal tubule is crucial for reabsorbing a significant portion of filtered solutes and water, utilizing both cellular and paracellular mechanisms.
keyPoints: Reabsorbs ~60% of filtered NaCl and water.
Reclaims ~90% of filtered bicarbonate.
Almost all glucose and amino acids are reabsorbed.
High-capacity fluid reabsorption facilitated by leaky tight junctions.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · SEGMENTAL NEPHRON FUNCTIONS | PROXIMAL TUBULO (part 1)

items.title: Mechanisms of Bicarbonate Reabsorption
items.content: Proximal tubular cells efficiently reclaim bicarbonate using a process dependent on carbonic anhydrases, with implications for renal bicarbonate excretion and therapeutic interventions.
keyPoints: Bicarbonate reabsorption is coupled to Na⁺ gradient via Na⁺/H⁺ exchange.
Carbonic anhydrase inhibitors like acetazolamide block this process.
Normal plasma bicarbonate levels: 24–26 meq/L.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · SEGMENTAL NEPHRON FUNCTIONS | PROXIMAL TUBULO (part 1)

items.title: Glucose Reabsorption and Glycosuria
items.content: The proximal tubule nearly completely reabsorbs glucose, with glycosuria occurring when plasma levels exceed a certain threshold.
keyPoints: Reabsorption of glucose is mediated by Na⁺-glucose cotransporters.
Glycosuria occurs at plasma glucose levels >180–200 mg/dL.
SLGT2 inhibitors can lower blood glucose in diabetes.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · SEGMENTAL NEPHRON FUNCTIONS | PROXIMAL TUBULO (part 1)

items.title: Drug Secretion and Serum Creatinine Levels
items.content: The proximal tubule secretes various organic acids, bases, and protein-bound drugs via specific transporters. Drugs like cimetidine and trimethoprim compete with endogenous compounds for these pathways, elevating serum creatinine levels without affecting GFR.
keyPoints: Specific transporters in the proximal tubule secrete organic acids (e.g., penicillins) and bases (e.g., dopamine).
P-glycoprotein secretes drugs like cyclosporine, digoxin, tacrolimus.
Cimetidine and trimethoprim compete with endogenous compounds for transport, elevating serum creatinine without changing GFR.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · PROXIMAL TUBULO (part 2)

items.title: Calcium and Phosphorus Homeostasis
items.content: The proximal tubule reabsorbs ~60-70% of filtered calcium and ~85% of filtered phosphorus, playing a crucial role in mineral balance. It also participates in endocrine pathways involving vitamin D activation and FGF23-mediated phosphate excretion.
keyPoints: Proximal tubule reabsorbs 60–70% of filtered calcium (passive diffusion) and ~85% of filtered phosphorus (sodium-coupled cotransport).
Vitamin D is activated by proximal tubular 1α-hydroxylase to calcitriol.
FGF23 suppresses sodium-phosphate cotransport, promoting renal phosphate excretion.
PTH stimulates vitamin D activation and suppresses sodium-phosphate cotransport.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · PROXIMAL TUBULO (part 2)

items.title: Role of Loop Diuretics and Mechanism of Action
items.content: Loop diuretics target the Na⁺/K⁺/2Cl⁻ cotransporter in the thick ascending limb, inhibiting NaCl reabsorption. This action disrupts countercurrent multiplication, reducing medullary hypertonicity and impairing urine concentration.
keyPoints: Approximately 15–25% of filtered NaCl is reabsorbed in the loop of Henle.
Na⁺/K⁺/2Cl⁻ cotransporter is the molecular target for loop diuretics.
Loop diuretics are potent agents affecting urine concentration by disrupting countercurrent multiplication.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · LOOP OF HENLE

items.title: Pathophysiology of Bartter's Syndrome
items.content: Bartter's syndrome results from loss-of-function mutations in genes encoding components of the Na⁺/K⁺/2Cl⁻ cotransporter or related channels, leading to salt-wasting renal disease with hypokalemia and metabolic alkalosis.
keyPoints: Loss-of-function mutations can affect NKCC2, KCNJ1, CLCNKB, BSND, or CASR genes.
Bartter's syndrome is characterized by hypokalemia and metabolic alkalosis due to impaired NaCl reabsorption.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · LOOP OF HENLE

items.title: Countercurrent Multiplication and Urine Concentration
items.content: The loop of Henle establishes a hypertonic medullary interstitium via countercurrent multiplication, crucial for water reabsorption in the inner medullary collecting duct. NaCl reabsorption without water dilutes tubular fluid, promoting solute trapping.
keyPoints: Countercurrent systems include the loop of Henle and vasa recta.
NaCl reabsorption by the thick ascending limb initiates countercurrent multiplication.
Highly water-permeable descending thin limb facilitates osmotic equilibrium, enhancing medullary hypertonicity.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · LOOP OF HENLE

items.title: Mechanism of NaCl Reabsorption in the Distal Convoluted Tubule
items.content: The distal convoluted tubule reabsorbs approximately 5% of filtered NaCl through a thiazide-sensitive Na⁺/Cl⁻ cotransporter, which works alongside basolateral Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase and Cl⁻ channels.
keyPoints: Reabsorption of ~5% of filtered NaCl
Thiazide-sensitive Na⁺/Cl⁻ cotransporter involved
Associated with basolateral Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase and Cl⁻ channels
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · DISTAL CONVOLUTED TUBULE

items.title: Gitelman Syndrome: Genetic Basis and Clinical Features
items.content: Loss-of-function mutations in SLC12A3, encoding the apical Na⁺/Cl⁻ cotransporter, result in Gitelman syndrome. This disorder is characterized by salt-wasting, hypokalemic alkalosis, and hypocalciuria.
keyPoints: SLC12A3 gene mutations cause Gitelman syndrome
Characterized by salt-wasting and hypokalemic alkalosis
Associated with hypocalciuria
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · DISTAL CONVOLUTED TUBULE

items.title: Role of Vasopressin in Water Reabsorption
items.content: Vasopressin regulates water permeability and reabsorption in the collecting duct by binding to V2 receptors, triggering a signaling cascade that inserts aquaporins into the apical membrane.
keyPoints: Vasopressin binds to V2 receptor on basolateral membrane of collecting duct cells.
Signaling through G protein-mediated activation of adenylyl cyclase increases cyclic AMP.
Aquaporin-2 is inserted into apical membrane, increasing water permeability and reabsorption.
In absence of vasopressin, urine remains dilute.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · COLLECTING DUCT (part 1)

items.title: Impact of Aldosterone on Sodium and Potassium Handling
items.content: Aldosterone modulates sodium reabsorption and potassium secretion in principal cells via the ENaC channel, influencing blood pressure and electrolyte balance.
keyPoints: Principal cells are key sites for aldosterone action.
ENaC mediates passive apical Na+ entry; basolateral exit by Na+/K+-ATPase.
Aldosterone increases gene transcription for Na+ reabsorption and K+ secretion.
Mutations in ENaC can cause Liddle's syndrome with hypokalemia, hypertension, and metabolic alkalosis.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · COLLECTING DUCT (part 1)

items.title: Mechanisms of Potassium Secretion
items.content: Potassium secretion in principal cells is driven by a concentration gradient and electrical gradient established by Na+/K+-ATPase, influenced by aldosterone.
keyPoints: High intracellular K+ concentration drives secretion into tubular fluid.
Negative lumen potential relative to cell interior favors K+ secretion.
Aldosterone enhances Na+ transport, increasing lumen electronegativity and promoting K+ secretion.
Potassium-sparing diuretics block ENaC, reducing Na+ reabsorption without causing hyperkalemia.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · COLLECTING DUCT (part 1)

items.title: Role of Intercalated Cells in Acid-Base Balance
items.content: Intercalated cells in the collecting duct are crucial for maintaining acid-base balance. Type A intercalated cells secrete H⁺ via an apical proton pump and reabsorb bicarbonate through a basolateral Cl⁻/HCO₃⁻ exchanger, while type B intercalated cells do the opposite. Aldosterone increases H⁺-ATPase pumps in type A cells, potentially leading to metabolic alkalosis.
keyPoints: Type A intercalated cells secrete acid and reabsorb bicarbonate.
Type B intercalated cells secrete bicarbonate and reabsorb acid.
Aldosterone increases H⁺-ATPase pumps in type A cells, contributing to metabolic alkalosis.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · COLLECTING DUCT (part 2)

items.title: Function of Inner Medullary Collecting Duct Cells
items.content: Inner medullary collecting duct cells facilitate Na⁺ reabsorption and K⁺ secretion via apical channels. Their activity is modulated by natriuretic peptides like atrial natriuretic peptide and urodilatin, which inhibit Na⁺ reabsorption through cGMP-mediated pathways, promoting natriuresis.
keyPoints: Apical Na⁺ and K⁺ channels mediate Na⁺ reabsorption and K⁺ secretion.
Natriuretic peptides (atrial natriuretic peptide and urodilatin) inhibit Na⁺ reabsorption via cGMP pathways.
Urea transport by these cells contributes to the hypertonicity of the medullary interstitium.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · COLLECTING DUCT (part 2)

items.subtopic: HORMONAL REGULATION OF SODIUM AND WATER BALANCE | WATER BALANCE
items.title: Role of Vasopressin in Osmoregulation and Water Balance
items.content: Vasopressin, released from the posterior pituitary gland, plays a crucial role in maintaining water balance by regulating renal water excretion. It is primarily stimulated by changes in plasma tonicity, with its release increasing linearly as plasma tonicity rises above normal (~280 mosmol/L). This mechanism ensures protection against dehydration and water intoxication.
keyPoints: Normal tonicity is ~280 mosmol/L and is rigorously defended to prevent cell dysfunction due to shrinkage or swelling.
Vasopressin release increases linearly with rising plasma tonicity above normal levels.
Osmoregulation involves TRPV1, 2, or 4 channels that respond to changes in tonicity by altering cell volume and Ca2+ concentration.
Only osmoreceptive TRPV neuronal cells modulate vasopressin release due to their neural connectivity and proximity to a minimal blood-brain barrier.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · HORMONAL REGULATION OF SODIUM AND WATER BALANCE | WATER BALANCE

items.subtopic: HORMONAL REGULATION OF SODIUM AND WATER BALANCE | WATER BALANCE
items.title: Kidney's Role in Water Balance through Urine Concentration
items.content: The kidneys regulate water balance by adjusting urine concentration. They can concentrate urine to an osmolality exceeding plasma or produce dilute urine to excrete excess water. This process is facilitated by aquaporins, with aquaporin-1 being active in all water-permeable segments and aquaporin-2, -3, and -4 promoting vasopressin-regulated permeability in the collecting duct.
keyPoints: Kidneys maintain water balance by regulating renal water excretion.
Urine can be concentrated to an osmolality exceeding that of plasma for water conservation or more dilute than plasma for excess water excretion.
Aquaporin-1 is constitutively active in all water-permeable segments like the proximal tubule and descending thin limb of the loop of Henle.
Aquaporins -2, -3, and -4 in the collecting duct are involved in vasopressin-regulated water permeability.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · HORMONAL REGULATION OF SODIUM AND WATER BALANCE | WATER BALANCE

items.title: Mechanisms Regulating Sodium Balance
items.content: Sodium balance is crucial for maintaining extracellular blood volume, influenced by arterial tone, cardiac stroke volume, heart rate, and the water and solute content of extracellular fluid. The renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system (RAAS) plays a pivotal role in modulating renal sodium excretion.
keyPoints: Sodium and accompanying anions are the most abundant extracellular effective osmoles, supporting blood volume regulation.
The balance between daily sodium intake and excretion is influenced by baroreceptors, atrial natriuretic peptides, RAAS, calcium signaling, adenosine, vasopressin, and neural adrenergic axis.
Renin, secreted by granular cells in the afferent arteriole wall, is controlled by β₁-adrenergic stimulation, macula densa input, and prostaglandins.
Angiotensin II promotes renal sodium and water reabsorption directly and indirectly, including stimulating aldosterone secretion.
Aldosterone increases ENaC activity in collecting duct principal cells via SGK1-mediated phosphorylation of Nedd4-2.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · SODIUM BALANCE

items.title: Role of Aldosterone in Sodium Reabsorption
items.content: Aldosterone, secreted by the adrenal cortex, binds to mineralocorticoid receptors in collecting duct cells, enhancing sodium reabsorption through increased ENaC activity.
keyPoints: Aldosterone is synthesized and secreted by granulosa cells in the adrenal cortex.
It binds to cytoplasmic mineralocorticoid receptors in collecting duct principal cells.
Increases ENaC, apical membrane K⁺ channel, and basolateral Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase activity.
SGK1 mediates phosphorylation of Nedd4-2, reducing its interaction with ENaC, increasing channel density at the plasma membrane.
Chronic aldosterone exposure leads to 'aldosterone escape' due to lower proximal tubular sodium reabsorption after blood volume expansion.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · SODIUM BALANCE

items.title: Aldosterone Escape and Its Clinical Implications
items.content: 'Aldosterone escape' is a phenomenon where chronic aldosterone exposure initially reduces urinary sodium excretion, but this effect diminishes as proximal tubular sodium reabsorption decreases.
keyPoints: Chronic aldosterone exposure results in lower urinary sodium excretion for a few days before returning to baseline levels.
'Aldosterone escape' occurs due to reduced proximal tubular sodium reabsorption following blood volume expansion.
Excess sodium overwhelms the reabsorptive capacity of distal nephron segments, facilitating this escape.

Atrial natriuretic peptides may facilitate 'aldosterone escape,' but their effectiveness is diminished in heart failure, nephrotic syndrome, and cirrhosis, leading to severe sodium retention and volume overload.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · SODIUM BALANCE

items.subtopic: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney | EMBRYOLOGIC DEVELOPMENT
items.question: Which factor guides the penetration of ureteric buds into the bilateral metanephric blastema during kidney development?
options: Morphogenic cues, cilia-derived planar cell polarity, and epigenetic marks
Vascular endothelial growth factor A (VEGF-A) only
The number of branching events in the ureteric buds
items.explanation: Morphogenic cues, cilia-derived planar cell polarity, and epigenetic marks guide the penetration of ureteric buds into the metanephric blastema.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney | EMBRYOLOGIC DEVELOPMENT

items.subtopic: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney | EMBRYOLOGIC DEVELOPMENT
items.question: What is the role of VEGF-A in glomerular development?
options: Induces mesenchymal epithelial transitions to form nephrons
Guides penetrating endothelial cells to form capillaries with surrounding mesangial cells
Determines the number of branching events in ureteric buds
Forms a filter for plasma water and solute by interacting with nephrin
items.explanation: VEGF-A guides penetrating endothelial cells to form capillaries with surrounding mesangial cells.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney | EMBRYOLOGIC DEVELOPMENT

items.subtopic: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney | EMBRYOLOGIC DEVELOPMENT
items.question: Which type of nephrons have long loops of Henle?
items.explanation: Juxtamedullary nephrons have long loops of Henle.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney | EMBRYOLOGIC DEVELOPMENT

items.subtopic: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney | EMBRYOLOGIC DEVELOPMENT
items.question: What is a consequence of impaired podocyte replenishment in the kidney?
options: Enhanced osmotic gradient for urine concentration
items.explanation: Impaired podocyte replenishment results in heavy proteinuria.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney | EMBRYOLOGIC DEVELOPMENT

items.subtopic: DETERMINANTS AND REGULATION OF GLOMERULAR FILTRATION
items.question: Which of the following is primarily responsible for opposing the hydrostatic pressure gradient in glomerular filtration?
options: Oncotic pressure within the glomerular capillary lumen
items.explanation: Oncotic pressure within the glomerular capillary lumen opposes the hydrostatic pressure gradient and affects filtration.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · DETERMINANTS AND REGULATION OF GLOMERULAR FILTRATION

items.subtopic: DETERMINANTS AND REGULATION OF GLOMERULAR FILTRATION
items.question: What is the primary driving force for glomerular filtration?
options: Hydrostatic pressure gradient across the glomerular capillary wall
items.explanation: The hydrostatic pressure gradient across the glomerular capillary wall is the primary driving force for glomerular filtration.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · DETERMINANTS AND REGULATION OF GLOMERULAR FILTRATION

items.subtopic: DETERMINANTS AND REGULATION OF GLOMERULAR FILTRATION
items.question: Which of the following factors contributes to autoregulation of glomerular filtration rate (GFR) by modulating afferent arteriolar tone?
options: Angiotensin II-mediated vasoconstriction of the efferent arteriole
Autonomous vasoreactive (myogenic) reflex in the afferent arteriole
items.explanation: The myogenic reflex is an autonomous vasoreactive response that modulates afferent arteriolar tone as part of GFR autoregulation.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · DETERMINANTS AND REGULATION OF GLOMERULAR FILTRATION

items.subtopic: DETERMINANTS AND REGULATION OF GLOMERULAR FILTRATION
items.question: Which component mediates the tubuloglomerular feedback by acting as sensors of solute concentration and tubular fluid flow rate?
options: Macula densa cells in the thick ascending limb of the loop of Henle
items.explanation: The macula densa, located in the thick ascending limb of the loop of Henle, mediates tubuloglomerular feedback.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · DETERMINANTS AND REGULATION OF GLOMERULAR FILTRATION

items.subtopic: MECHANISMS OF RENAL TUBULAR TRANSPORT | EPITHELIAL SOLUTE TRANSPORT
items.question: Which feature allows renal epithelial cells to be classified as polarized?
options: The presence of tight junctions forming an occlusive barrier.
The asymmetric assignment of membrane proteins and lipids.
The sequential movement of solutes across cell membranes.
The robust paracellular transport through leaky epithelia.
items.explanation: Polarization is due to the asymmetric allocation of membrane proteins and lipids.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · MECHANISMS OF RENAL TUBULAR TRANSPORT | EPITHELIAL SOLUTE TRANSPORT

items.subtopic: MECHANISMS OF RENAL TUBULAR TRANSPORT | EPITHELIAL SOLUTE TRANSPORT
items.question: What type of epithelia allows for more refined control and regulation of transport?
options: Leaky epithelia with low electrical resistance.
Tight epithelia with high electrical resistance.
items.explanation: Tight epithelia, found in distal nephron segments like the collecting duct, allow for refined control and regulation of transport.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · MECHANISMS OF RENAL TUBULAR TRANSPORT | EPITHELIAL SOLUTE TRANSPORT

items.subtopic: MECHANISMS OF RENAL TUBULAR TRANSPORT | EPITHELIAL SOLUTE TRANSPORT
items.question: Which type of epithelia is most suited for bulk fluid reabsorption?
options: Tight epithelia with high electrical resistance.
Leaky epithelia with low electrical resistance.
items.explanation: Leaky epithelia, such as those in the proximal tubule, are suited for bulk fluid reabsorption.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · MECHANISMS OF RENAL TUBULAR TRANSPORT | EPITHELIAL SOLUTE TRANSPORT

items.question: Which type of transport requires metabolic energy generated by the hydrolysis of ATP?
items.explanation: Active transport requires metabolic energy from ATP to move ions against their concentration gradient.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · MEMBRANE TRANSPORT

items.question: Which of the following is an example of a transporter that mediates glucose transport by tubular cells?
items.explanation: GLUT2 is a hexose transporter that mediates glucose transport by tubular cells.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · MEMBRANE TRANSPORT

items.question: What type of transport involves the movement of solutes through membrane proteins driven by favorable concentration gradients?
items.explanation: Facilitated diffusion is a type of passive transport mediated by carriers or uniporters, driven by concentration gradients.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · MEMBRANE TRANSPORT

items.question: Which of the following mechanisms is primarily responsible for maintaining a steep Na⁺ gradient in proximal tubular cells?
items.explanation: The basolateral Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase maintains a low intracellular Na⁺ concentration, establishing the gradient.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · SEGMENTAL NEPHRON FUNCTIONS | PROXIMAL TUBULO (part 1)

items.question: What is the primary role of carbonic anhydrases in bicarbonate reabsorption in the proximal tubule?
options: They convert filtered bicarbonate to water and CO₂.
They transport bicarbonate across basolateral membranes.
items.explanation: Carbonic anhydrases metabolize carbonic acid into water and CO₂, facilitating bicarbonate reabsorption.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · SEGMENTAL NEPHRON FUNCTIONS | PROXIMAL TUBULO (part 1)

items.question: Which transporter is involved in the cellular pathway of Cl⁻ reabsorption in later segments of the proximal tubule?
items.explanation: The basolateral Cl⁻ exit is mediated by a K⁺/Cl⁻ cotransporter.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · SEGMENTAL NEPHRON FUNCTIONS | PROXIMAL TUBULO (part 1)

items.question: What condition can lead to glycosuria due to saturation of glucose transport mechanisms in the proximal tubule?
items.explanation: Glycosuria occurs when plasma glucose levels exceed the transport maximum, as seen in untreated diabetes.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · SEGMENTAL NEPHRON FUNCTIONS | PROXIMAL TUBULO (part 1)

items.question: Which transporter is responsible for secreting several medically important drugs, including cyclosporine and digoxin?
items.explanation: The ATP-dependent transporter P-glycoprotein encoded by ABCB1 secretes several medically important drugs.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · PROXIMAL TUBULO (part 2)

items.question: What is the primary route of calcium reabsorption in the proximal tubule?
options: Passive diffusion through the paracellular route
items.explanation: Calcium reabsorption in the proximal tubule is mostly by passive diffusion through the paracellular route.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · PROXIMAL TUBULO (part 2)

items.question: Which hormone suppresses sodium-phosphate cotransport and promotes renal phosphate excretion?
items.explanation: Fibroblast growth hormone 23 (FGF23) suppresses sodium-phosphate cotransport and promotes renal phosphate excretion.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · PROXIMAL TUBULO (part 2)

items.question: Which segment of the loop of Henle is primarily responsible for NaCl reabsorption?
items.explanation: Approximately 15–25% of filtered NaCl is reabsorbed in the loop of Henle, mainly by the thick ascending limb.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · LOOP OF HENLE

items.question: What molecular target do loop diuretics act on?
items.explanation: The Na⁺/K⁺/2Cl⁻ cotransporter on the apical membrane of the thick ascending limb is the molecular target for loop diuretics.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · LOOP OF HENLE

items.question: What condition is associated with loss-of-function mutations in the Na⁺/K⁺/2Cl⁻ cotransporter (NKCC2) or related genes?
items.explanation: Loss-of-function mutations in one of five distinct genes encoding components of the Na⁺/K⁺/2Cl⁻ cotransporter can cause Bartter's syndrome.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · LOOP OF HENLE

items.question: What mechanism contributes to a positive electrostatic charge in the lumen relative to the interstitium?
options: Potassium recycling through an apical potassium channel
items.explanation: Potassium recycling contributes to a positive electrostatic charge in the lumen, promoting divalent cation reabsorption.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · LOOP OF HENLE

items.question: Which transporter in the distal convoluted tubule is sensitive to thiazide diuretics and involved in Na⁺/Cl⁻ reabsorption?
items.explanation: The thiazide-sensitive Na⁺/Cl⁻ cotransporter is responsible for Na⁺/Cl⁻ reabsorption in the distal convoluted tubule.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · DISTAL CONVOLUTED TUBULE

items.question: What condition results from loss-of-function mutations of SLC12A3 encoding the apical Na⁺/Cl⁻ cotransporter?
items.explanation: Loss-of-function mutations of SLC12A3 cause Gitelman syndrome, a salt-wasting disorder.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · DISTAL CONVOLUTED TUBULE

items.question: Which protein complex is critical for Mg²⁺ reabsorption in the distal convoluted tubule?
items.explanation: The molecular complex of TRPM6 and TRPM7 proteins is critical for Mg²⁺ reabsorption in the distal convoluted tubule.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · DISTAL CONVOLUTED TUBULE

items.question: Which hormone regulates the insertion of aquaporin-2 into the apical membrane of collecting duct cells?
items.explanation: Vasopressin binds to V2 receptors and triggers the insertion of aquaporin-2 into the apical membrane, enhancing water reabsorption.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · COLLECTING DUCT (part 1)

items.question: What is the primary function of principal cells in the cortical collecting duct?
options: Water and Na⁺ reabsorption, K⁺ secretion
items.explanation: Principal cells are responsible for water and Na⁺ reabsorption, as well as K⁺ secretion.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · COLLECTING DUCT (part 1)

items.question: What effect do nonpeptide V2 receptor blockers have on urine production?
items.explanation: Nonpeptide V2 receptor blockers antagonize vasopressin's effect, leading to increased urine production (water diuresis).
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · COLLECTING DUCT (part 1)

items.question: Which enzyme is involved in the activation of ENaC in nephrotic syndrome?
items.explanation: In nephrotic syndrome, plasmin activates ENaC, leading to increased Na⁺ reabsorption.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · COLLECTING DUCT (part 1)

items.question: Which type of intercalated cell is primarily involved in acid secretion?
items.explanation: Type A intercalated cells have an apical proton pump that mediates acid secretion.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · COLLECTING DUCT (part 2)

items.question: What is the effect of aldosterone on type A intercalated cells?
options: Increases H⁺-ATPase pumps, contributing to metabolic alkalosis
items.explanation: Aldosterone increases the number of H⁺-ATPase pumps, sometimes contributing to metabolic alkalosis.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · COLLECTING DUCT (part 2)

items.question: Which type of intercalated cell predominates in states of bicarbonate excess with alkalemia?
items.explanation: In states of bicarbonate excess with alkalemia, type B intercalated cells predominate.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · COLLECTING DUCT (part 2)

items.subtopic: HORMONAL REGULATION OF SODIUM AND WATER BALANCE | WATER BALANCE
items.question: Which of the following channels are osmoreceptive and modulate vasopressin release in response to changes in tonicity?
options: TRPV1, TRPV2, and TRPV4 neuronal cells connected to the organum vasculosum of the lamina terminalis
All cells expressing mechanosensitive TRPV channels
Cells expressing aquaporins in the kidney
Cells in the proximal tubule and descending thin limb of the loop of Henle
items.explanation: Only TRPV1, TRPV2, or TRPV4 neuronal cells connected to the organum vasculosum of the lamina terminalis are osmoreceptive.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · HORMONAL REGULATION OF SODIUM AND WATER BALANCE | WATER BALANCE

items.subtopic: HORMONAL REGULATION OF SODIUM AND WATER BALANCE | WATER BALANCE
items.question: What is the primary stimulus for the release of vasopressin from the posterior pituitary gland?
items.explanation: The primary stimulus for vasopressin release is changing tonicity.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · HORMONAL REGULATION OF SODIUM AND WATER BALANCE | WATER BALANCE

items.subtopic: HORMONAL REGULATION OF SODIUM AND WATER BALANCE | WATER BALANCE
items.question: Which aquaporins are involved in vasopressin-regulated water permeability in the collecting duct?
items.explanation: Aquaporins involved in vasopressin-regulated water permeability in the collecting duct are aquaporin-2, -3, and -4.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · HORMONAL REGULATION OF SODIUM AND WATER BALANCE | WATER BALANCE

items.question: Which system is primarily responsible for modulating renal sodium excretion?
items.explanation: The renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system is the best-understood hormonal system modulating renal Na⁺ excretion.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · SODIUM BALANCE

items.question: What triggers aldosterone escape after chronic exposure to aldosterone?
options: Lower proximal tubular sodium reabsorption following blood volume expansion
Enhanced effectiveness of atrial natriuretic peptides
items.explanation: Aldosterone escape is explained by lower proximal tubular Na⁺ reabsorption following blood volume expansion.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · SODIUM BALANCE

items.question: Which peptide counterbalances several actions of angiotensin II on blood pressure and renal function?
items.explanation: Alternative metabolism of angiotensin by ACE2 generates the vasodilatory peptide angiotensin 1-7 that counterbalances several actions of angiotensin II.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · SODIUM BALANCE

items.question: How does aldosterone increase the activity of ENaC in collecting duct principal cells?
options: By stimulating transcription of SGK1, which phosphorylates Nedd4-2
Directly binding to and activating ENaC channels
Inhibiting renin secretion through a negative feedback loop
Increasing the synthesis of angiotensin II
items.explanation: Aldosterone-stimulated transcription of SGK1 leads to phosphorylation of Nedd4-2, increasing ENaC activity.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · SODIUM BALANCE

items.subtopic: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney | EMBRYOLOGIC DEVELOPMENT
items.statement: The majority of nephrons are juxtamedullary, with glomeruli located at the boundary of the cortex and outer medulla.
items.explanation: Most nephrons are cortical, not juxtamedullary.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney | EMBRYOLOGIC DEVELOPMENT

items.subtopic: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney | EMBRYOLOGIC DEVELOPMENT
items.statement: Mesangial cells make contact with glomerular capillary loops and help maintain their arrangement.
items.explanation: Mesangial cells do make contact with glomerular capillaries and help maintain their structure.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney | EMBRYOLOGIC DEVELOPMENT

items.subtopic: DETERMINANTS AND REGULATION OF GLOMERULAR FILTRATION
items.statement: Autoregulation of glomerular filtration rate (GFR) is achieved solely through changes in renal artery pressure.
items.explanation: Autoregulation involves multiple mechanisms including myogenic reflex, tubuloglomerular feedback, and angiotensin II-mediated vasoconstriction.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · DETERMINANTS AND REGULATION OF GLOMERULAR FILTRATION

items.subtopic: DETERMINANTS AND REGULATION OF GLOMERULAR FILTRATION
items.statement: Angiotensin II causes vasoconstriction of the afferent arteriole to regulate glomerular filtration.
items.explanation: Angiotensin II primarily causes vasoconstriction of the efferent arteriole, increasing glomerular hydrostatic pressure.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · DETERMINANTS AND REGULATION OF GLOMERULAR FILTRATION

items.subtopic: MECHANISMS OF RENAL TUBULAR TRANSPORT | EPITHELIAL SOLUTE TRANSPORT
items.statement: Tight junctions in renal epithelial cells form a completely occlusive barrier.
items.explanation: Tight junctions are not completely occlusive, allowing for paracellular transport.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · MECHANISMS OF RENAL TUBULAR TRANSPORT | EPITHELIAL SOLUTE TRANSPORT

items.subtopic: MECHANISMS OF RENAL TUBULAR TRANSPORT | EPITHELIAL SOLUTE TRANSPORT
items.statement: The proximal tubule contains tight epithelia.
items.explanation: The proximal tubule contains leaky epithelia, suitable for bulk fluid reabsorption.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · MECHANISMS OF RENAL TUBULAR TRANSPORT | EPITHELIAL SOLUTE TRANSPORT

items.statement: The Na+/K+-ATPase pump moves ions against their chemical gradient using ATP.
items.explanation: Na+/K+-ATPase is an active transport pump that uses ATP to move Na+ and K+ ions against their gradients.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · MEMBRANE TRANSPORT

items.statement: Secondary active transport does not rely on the energy from ATP hydrolysis.
items.explanation: Secondary active transport uses the potential energy stored in ion concentration gradients, not direct ATP hydrolysis.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · MEMBRANE TRANSPORT

items.statement: Proximal tubular cells reabsorb nearly all filtered bicarbonate through a process dependent on carbonic anhydrases.
items.explanation: Bicarbonate reabsorption is mediated by carbonic anhydrase activity in proximal tubules.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · SEGMENTAL NEPHRON FUNCTIONS | PROXIMAL TUBULO (part 1)

items.statement: Chloride reabsorption in the proximal tubule is primarily through Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase activity.
items.explanation: Cl⁻ reabsorption involves apical exchange and basolateral K⁺/Cl⁻ cotransport, not Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · SEGMENTAL NEPHRON FUNCTIONS | PROXIMAL TUBULO (part 1)

items.statement: Mutations in SLC3A1 or SLC7A9 genes cause cystinuria by impairing the reabsorption of certain amino acids.
items.explanation: Cystinuria is caused by mutations that impair reabsorption of specific amino acids due to defective transporters encoded by these genes.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · PROXIMAL TUBULO (part 2)

items.statement: PTH increases sodium-phosphate cotransport in the proximal tubule.
items.explanation: PTH actually suppresses sodium-phosphate cotransport, not increases it.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · PROXIMAL TUBULO (part 2)

items.statement: The descending thin limb of the loop of Henle is impermeable to water.
items.explanation: The descending thin limb is highly permeable to water due to aquaporin-1 channels.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · LOOP OF HENLE

items.statement: Countercurrent multiplication in the loop of Henle helps maintain a hypertonic medullary interstitium.
items.explanation: The countercurrent flow in the loop of Henle and vasa recta maintains the hypertonic environment of the inner medulla.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · LOOP OF HENLE

items.statement: Blocking apical Na⁺/Cl⁻ cotransport will reduce intracellular Na⁺, favoring increased basolateral Na⁺/Ca²⁺ exchange and passive apical Ca²⁺ entry.
items.explanation: Reduced intracellular Na⁺ from blocking the Na⁺/Cl⁻ cotransporter enhances Na⁺/Ca²⁺ exchange, increasing Ca²⁺ reabsorption.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · DISTAL CONVOLUTED TUBULE

items.statement: PTH inhibits calcium reabsorption in the distal convoluted tubule.
items.explanation: PTH stimulates, rather than inhibits, calcium reabsorption in the distal convoluted tubule.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · DISTAL CONVOLUTED TUBULE

items.statement: Aldosterone increases K⁺ secretion in principal cells.
items.explanation: Aldosterone enhances Na⁺ reabsorption, which creates an electrical gradient favoring K⁺ secretion.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · COLLECTING DUCT (part 1)

items.statement: Type B intercalated cells mediate acid secretion.
items.explanation: Type B intercalated cells mediate bicarbonate secretion and acid reabsorption, not acid secretion.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · COLLECTING DUCT (part 1)

items.statement: Atrial natriuretic peptides are secreted by renal tubular epithelia.
items.explanation: Atrial natriuretic peptides are secreted by atrial myocytes in response to volume expansion.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · COLLECTING DUCT (part 2)

items.statement: Urea is recycled by diffusing from the interstitium into the descending and ascending limbs of the loop of Henle.
items.explanation: Urea is indeed recycled by diffusing from the interstitium into these parts of the nephron.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · COLLECTING DUCT (part 2)

items.subtopic: HORMONAL REGULATION OF SODIUM AND WATER BALANCE | WATER BALANCE
items.statement: The kidneys can produce urine with an osmolality lower than that of plasma.
items.explanation: The kidneys can produce dilute urine to excrete excess water.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · HORMONAL REGULATION OF SODIUM AND WATER BALANCE | WATER BALANCE

items.subtopic: HORMONAL REGULATION OF SODIUM AND WATER BALANCE | WATER BALANCE
items.statement: Cellular concentrations of Na+ are the primary determinant of tonicity.
items.explanation: While Na+ concentration is a surrogate marker, cellular concentrations of K+ also play a role in determining tonicity.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · HORMONAL REGULATION OF SODIUM AND WATER BALANCE | WATER BALANCE

items.statement: Angiotensin II directly increases sodium reabsorption in the proximal tubule.
items.explanation: Stimulation of proximal tubular Na⁺/H⁺ exchange by angiotensin II directly increases Na⁺ reabsorption.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · SODIUM BALANCE

items.statement: Atrial natriuretic peptides are more effective in heart failure, nephrotic syndrome, and cirrhosis.
items.explanation: Atrial natriuretic peptides lose their effectiveness in these clinical settings, leading to severe Na⁺ retention.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · SODIUM BALANCE

items.subtopic: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney | EMBRYOLOGIC DEVELOPMENT
items.assertion: Juxtamedullary nephrons are responsible for creating an osmotic gradient necessary for urine concentration.
items.reason: Juxtamedullary nephrons have long loops of Henle that extend deep into the medulla.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney | EMBRYOLOGIC DEVELOPMENT

items.subtopic: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney | EMBRYOLOGIC DEVELOPMENT
items.assertion: The number of nephrons in a kidney is determined by the branching events of ureteric buds.
items.reason: Each branch of a ureteric bud produces a new set of nephrons.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney | EMBRYOLOGIC DEVELOPMENT

items.subtopic: DETERMINANTS AND REGULATION OF GLOMERULAR FILTRATION
items.assertion: The myogenic reflex helps protect the glomerular capillary from sudden changes in systolic pressure.
items.reason: Acute changes in renal perfusion pressure evoke reflex constriction or dilatation of the afferent arteriole.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · DETERMINANTS AND REGULATION OF GLOMERULAR FILTRATION

items.subtopic: DETERMINANTS AND REGULATION OF GLOMERULAR FILTRATION
items.assertion: Tubuloglomerular feedback decreases glomerular filtration rate (GFR) by dilating the afferent arteriole.
items.reason: High solute delivery to the macula densa evokes vasoconstriction of the afferent arteriole.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because TGF decreases GFR by constricting, not dilating, the afferent arteriole.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · DETERMINANTS AND REGULATION OF GLOMERULAR FILTRATION

items.subtopic: MECHANISMS OF RENAL TUBULAR TRANSPORT | EPITHELIAL SOLUTE TRANSPORT
items.assertion: The proximal tubule is characterized by low-resistance epithelia.
items.reason: Low-resistance epithelia are associated with robust paracellular transport.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why the proximal tubule has low-resistance epithelia.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · MECHANISMS OF RENAL TUBULAR TRANSPORT | EPITHELIAL SOLUTE TRANSPORT

items.assertion: Proximal tubular cells reabsorb ~60% of filtered NaCl and water.
items.reason: This is due to the high-capacity fluid reabsorption enabled by leaky tight junctions and active transport mechanisms.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining the assertion.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · SEGMENTAL NEPHRON FUNCTIONS | PROXIMAL TUBULO (part 1)

items.assertion: Inhibitors of Na⁺/glucose cotransporter SGLT2 can lower blood glucose levels.
items.reason: These inhibitors block the reabsorption of glucose in the proximal tubule, leading to glycosuria.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining the assertion.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · SEGMENTAL NEPHRON FUNCTIONS | PROXIMAL TUBULO (part 1)

items.assertion: Impaired acidification of endocytic vesicles in the proximal tubule can lead to low-molecular-weight proteinuria.
items.reason: This impairment is due to pathogenic variants in CLCN5, which encodes a Cl-/H+ exchanger necessary for vesicle acidification.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · PROXIMAL TUBULO (part 2)

items.assertion: Bartter's syndrome is characterized by hypokalemia and metabolic alkalosis.
items.reason: Loss-of-function mutations in genes encoding components of the Na⁺/K⁺/2Cl⁻ cotransporter or related channels cause Bartter's syndrome.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · LOOP OF HENLE

items.assertion: The thick ascending limb is responsible for water reabsorption in the loop of Henle.
items.reason: Water permeability is negligible in the ascending thin and thick limbs.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the thick ascending limb is not responsible for water reabsorption; the reason is true.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · LOOP OF HENLE

items.assertion: Ca²⁺ reabsorption is inversely related to Na⁺ reabsorption.
items.reason: Blocking apical Na⁺/Cl⁻ cotransport increases basolateral Na⁺/Ca²⁺ exchange and passive apical Ca²⁺ entry.
items.explanation: The reason correctly explains the assertion as blocking Na⁺/Cl⁻ cotransport enhances Ca²⁺ reabsorption by increasing Na⁺/Ca²⁺ exchange.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · DISTAL CONVOLUTED TUBULE

items.reason: Blocking ENaC reduces Na⁺ reabsorption and prevents hypokalemia.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and amiloride's action on ENaC explains its effect on Na⁺ and K⁺ balance.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · COLLECTING DUCT (part 1)

items.reason: It promotes water reabsorption by inserting aquaporins into the apical membrane.
items.explanation: Both are true, and vasopressin's action on aquaporins explains its role in concentrating urine.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · COLLECTING DUCT (part 1)

items.subtopic: HORMONAL REGULATION OF SODIUM AND WATER BALANCE | WATER BALANCE
items.assertion: The release of vasopressin is stimulated primarily by changes in plasma tonicity.
items.reason: Vasopressin secretion increases linearly as plasma tonicity rises above normal.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · HORMONAL REGULATION OF SODIUM AND WATER BALANCE | WATER BALANCE

items.assertion: Chronic exposure to aldosterone results in sustained lower urinary sodium excretion.
items.reason: Aldosterone increases the activity of ENaC, leading to increased renal sodium reabsorption.
items.explanation: While aldosterone does increase ENaC activity, chronic exposure leads to aldosterone escape due to changes in proximal tubular Na⁺ reabsorption.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · SODIUM BALANCE

items.assertion: Angiotensin II promotes renal sodium and water reabsorption.
items.reason: It stimulates aldosterone secretion, which increases ENaC activity in the collecting duct.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Cell Biology and Physiology of the Kidney · SODIUM BALANCE
